summary: A biographical overview of René Descartes, detailing his early education, his realization that only mathematics offered certainty, and his subsequent quest to build a universal method of reasoning. It covers his major works, his integration of algebra and geometry, and his mechanistic view of the physical universe.

principles.statement: Seek knowledge only from within yourself or from the real world, rather than from traditional texts.
principles.rationale: Traditional schooling and Aristotelian logic lack certainty; true knowledge comes from direct experience and internal reflection.
principles.source_quote: I entirely abandoned the study of letters, resolving to seek no knowledge other than that which could be found in myself or else in the great book of the world.

principles.statement: Metaphysical truths can and should be proven with mathematical certainty.
principles.rationale: Mathematics is the only subject that provides satisfactory certainty, making it the ideal foundation for proving truths in physics and metaphysics.
principles.source_quote: At least I think that I have found how to prove metaphysical truths in a manner which is more evident than the proofs of geometry - in my opinion, that is

principles.statement: A universal discipline of human reason is the ultimate instrument of knowledge.
principles.rationale: Ordinary mathematics is just the 'outer garment' of a deeper, universal method of reasoning that can discover truths in any field whatsoever.
principles.source_quote: This discipline should contain the primary rudiments of human reason and extend to the discovery of truths in any field whatever. Frankly speaking, I am convinced that it is a more powerful instrument of knowledge than any other with which human beings are endowed, as it is the source of all the rest.

frameworks.name: Universal Science of Quantity
frameworks.when_to_apply: When attempting to solve any general question proposed about any kind of quantity, whether continuous or discrete.
steps: Identify the nature of the quantity being analyzed.
For discrete quantities (arithmetic), solve using rational numbers or surd numbers.
For continuous quantities, solve using straight or circular lines.
If the problem is more complex, solve using curves generated by a single continuous motion (traced by new compasses).
For the most complex problems, use curved lines generated by separate, non-subordinate motions.
frameworks.source_quote: [I want to promote a] completely new science by which all questions in general may be solved that can be proposed about any kind of quantity, continuous as well as discrete. But each according to its own nature.

mental_models.name: The 'New World' Hypothesis
mental_models.description: A rhetorical and philosophical lens used to explore controversial scientific ideas without directly challenging established dogma. By framing a physical theory as a hypothetical 'new world' created by God, one can freely express judgments about natural laws.
mental_models.example: Descartes used this model in 'Le Monde' after hearing of Galileo's condemnation, deciding to 'speak solely of what would happen in a new world, if God were now to create... and allow her to act in accordance with the laws He had established.'

mental_models.name: Contact Mechanics (Vortex Theory)
mental_models.description: The lens of viewing the universe as a plenum (completely filled with matter) where all physical forces and movements occur strictly through direct physical contact, rather than mysterious invisible forces.
mental_models.example: Descartes assumed the universe was filled with matter that settled into a system of vortices, which physically carried the sun, stars, planets, and comets in their paths, rejecting the idea of a vacuum or gravity acting at a distance.

heuristics: Base all knowledge on the certainty of mathematics.
Seek solitude and peace away from the distractions of the city to do deep work.

signature_quotes.text: I entirely abandoned the study of letters, resolving to seek no knowledge other than that which could be found in myself or else in the great book of the world.
signature_quotes.context: Written in 'Discourse on the Method' regarding his decision to abandon traditional schooling.

signature_quotes.text: At least I think that I have found how to prove metaphysical truths in a manner which is more evident than the proofs of geometry...
signature_quotes.context: From a letter to Mersenne in October 1629 regarding his work on the foundations of physics.

signature_quotes.text: This discipline should contain the primary rudiments of human reason and extend to the discovery of truths in any field whatever.
signature_quotes.context: From his posthumous manuscript 'Regulae ad directionem ingenii', describing his universal method.

anti_patterns.description: Believing in 'action at a distance' (such as gravity acting across empty space).
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Descartes believed there could be no vacuum around the Earth; forces can only be transferred through direct physical contact within a universe filled with matter.

anti_patterns.description: Relying on traditional Aristotelian logic and classical schooling to acquire knowledge.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It lacks certainty. Descartes found that his extensive schooling only made him understand how little he knew, leading him to rebuild knowledge on the foundation of mathematics.
